next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 5

By nine in the morning, the rain had already flooded the seam where Studio C met the loading dock. Water tracked in by couriers had made the linoleum shine like a tongue. The analytics room behind the prop cage smelled of hot fans, cardboard glue, and the sweet chemical stink from wigs that had dried overnight on foam heads. Ina sat with her knees under a folding desk and watched campaign dashboards update in stuttering increments.

The top line looked healthy: reach was up twenty-three percent in four weeks, rewatch loops were up in two border clusters, and fan-edit reposts were up enough to make the social team send fireworks emojis in the internal chat. Everything any executive sponsor wanted to see looked bright green.

She closed that tab and opened the raw export she had fought for since Monday. The sheet listed distribution IDs, shipment labels disguised as karaoke compilations and language drills, and device fingerprints from border-proximate handoffs. It was a broken spine of numbers that should have told a cleaner story than her body wanted to believe.

Kwon's instructions from the previous night were still pinned in her notes app: Audit reach. Isolate high-performing payloads. Scale what travels.

Nothing in that sentence asked what happened after arrival.

She had asked for Kang Minjae at 08:00 because no one else in the operation understood how those drives physically moved. At 08:47 he still was not there. At 08:51 her coffee had gone gray and cold. At 08:56 the door opened with a wet suction sound and he came in shaking rain from his hair like a...
open_hooks_to_carry: Writers-room argument establishes market pressure against overt politics.
Ina embeds separation grief into a sibling subplot and lullaby motif.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ar the back. She sat with the pen uncapped, listening to the fan belt cry in memory, hearing her father say recruited like it was a weather report and a warning both.

She wrote one sentence in Korean first, then below it in English because contract language had entered her head in English and she wanted no excuse to misunderstand herself.

No operation of mine will knowingly increase civilian harm.

She stared at it, added a date, then drew a box around the line as if a box could turn resolve into law.

After a moment she wrote two clarifications underneath, smaller, harsher:

No targeting civilians for pressure effects.

No concealment of civilian casualties if discovered.

The pen dug so hard on discovered that it almost tore the page.

She knew the rule had no enforcement mechanism. She had not told Kwon. She had not told anyone. Private ethics were easy to keep until invoices arrived and someone older called your compromise history.

Still, she wrote it.

Then she opened a new note on her phone, copied the same three lines, and buried the file under a grocery list title so it would not glow like a confession if someone glanced at her screen.

Outside, a siren moved along the river road and faded. Ina closed the notebook, slid it back into the rice bin, and turned off the kitchen light. In the dark, the apartment window held her reflection over the black water, a layered image: her face, the opposite bank's construction cranes, and the faint ghost of her mother's handwriting still imprinted behind her eyes.

She stayed in the operation.

She did not tell her father why.
state_deltas_required: Deliver a pilot rewrite that can carry reunification sentiment through commercial television.
Ina's compromise script is approved and Ji-eun is locked into a politically loaded role.
